coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence quenching of thermally treated waste-derived carbon dots for selective metal ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Chechia
Lin, Tzu-Jen
Huang, Ying-Chu
Chen, You-Yu
Wang, Ke-Hsuan
Andrew Lin, Kun-Yi
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, carbon-dots (CDs) were derived from the thermal oxidation of an agricultural waste, bitter tea residue, to obtain different sp2/sp3 ratios and electronic structures for metal sensing. The CDs obtained from calcination at 700 °C exhibited the highest photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 11.8% among all the samples treated at different temperatures. These CDs had a high degree of graphitization, which resulted in a strong π-π* electron transition, and hence in a high QY. The strong photoluminescence of the CDs could be used to sense the metal ions Ag+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Sn2+ by monitoring their PL intensity at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The metals inhibited the PL intensity in the order Ag+ > Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ > Sr2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, which demonstrated that the CDs exhibited high metal ion detection capability and selectivity. The detection of Fe3+ using CDs was performed in the range of 10–100 ppm with a LOD (limit of detection) value of 0.380 ppm. Theoretical calculations demonstrated that Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ induced charge transfer excitation and that Fe2+ and Ni2+ induced d-d transitions via complexation with the sp2 clusters. The charge transfer excitation and d-d transitions hindered the π-π* transition of the sp2 clusters, leading to a quenching effect. On the other hand, Li+, Na+, and K+ ions did not alter the π-π* transition of the sp2 clusters, resulting in a negligible quenching effect. In summary, the oxidation level and electronic structure of CDs derived from bitter tea residue could be tailored, and the CDs were shown to be a facile, sustainable, and eco-friendly material for metal sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bitter tea residue
Carbon dots
Metal sensing
Photoluminescence
DFT calculation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: carbonyl and carboxylate groups of BP
Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The structural features of the as-prepared BP and xBP were determined from their XRD patterns and FTIR spectra (Figure S1, Supporting Information). The XRD pattern of BP (Figure S1a) shows two peaks at 2θ values of approximately 15° and 22°. After thermal oxidation (xBP samples, x = 3, 5, 7), these peaks shift to lower and higher 2θ values, reaching approximately 11.5° and 28°, respectively. The shifts in these peaks suggested the presence of graphitic carbon (at ca. 28°), which originated in part from thermal oxidation. Figure S1b shows the FTIR spectra of the xBP samples, which confirm the presence of C[dbnd]C, C–C, and C–O (epoxy) groups (Walekar et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2012).Fig. 2c shows the survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples. The survey spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples are composed mainly of C 1s and O 1s, with minor N and S signals (Fig. 2c and Table S2). The nitrogen contents of these samples are lower than 2%, whereas the carbon and oxygen contents increased and decreased, respectively, with increasing thermal oxidation temperature (Table S2), indicating successful carbonization. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples can be fitted to three peaks at 284.7, 285.7, and 287.8 eV, which were assigned to C–C/C[dbnd]C bonds (sp2 and sp3 structure), C–O bonds, and O[dbnd]C–OH groups (carboxylate group) (Weigend and Ahlrichs, 2005), respectively (Fig. 2d). In agreement with the previous FTIR analysis, thermal oxidation was found to result in the formation of graphitic carbon domains to form CDs by interconnecting the carbonyl and carboxylate groups of BP. Among the xBP-D samples, 7BP-D exhibited the highest degree of graphitization (69.2% graphitic carbon and 30.8% oxygenated carbon), indicating its more organized sp2/sp3 connections and greater retention of carbon rings in the basal plane, which is advantageous for the π-π* electron transition and hence for achieving high conductivity (Yang et al., 2018; Yeh et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018). On the other hand, the small peak located at approximately 293 eV, which was assigned as a shake-up peak (characteristic of conjugated systems or aromatic groups) (Zhang et al., 2013a), increased with the thermal oxidation temperature. This result supports the above conclusion that the oxidation temperature can effectively improve the carbonization degree and hence stabilize the aromatic unit. Fig. 2e shows the high-resolution N 1s spectra, which reveals that only trace N was detected. Fig. 2f presents the high-resolution O 1s spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples. The fitted peaks at approximately 531.4 and 533.4 eV were attributed to C[dbnd]O and C–OH bonds, consistent with the above FTIR and high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Cheng et al., 2019). Based on the above analysis, the xBP-D obtained in this study exhibited a high degree of carbonization with many C[dbnd]C and C–C bonds, as well as a certain fraction of C–O (epoxy) groups, within its aromatic units. The functional groups attached to the surface of CDs and the degree of carbonization of the CDs were strongly correlated with their thermal oxidation temperature, which could thus be an important parameter to govern their PL emission and metal sensing properties.This research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 107-2221-E-011-156-MY3, 108-2221-E-011-165-MY3, 108-2221-E-033-027-MY3).
This research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan ( MOST 107-2221-E-011-156-MY3, 108-2221-E-011-165-MY3, 108-2221-E-033-027-MY3 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for xBP-D (x = 3, 5, and 7, corresponding to thermal oxidation at 300 °C, 500 °C, or 700 °C) from bitter tea residue (BP). (b) sp2 cluster model, C46, C60, and C114, which correspond to the 7BP-D, 5BP-D, and 3BP-D samples in this study, respectively.

(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples (x = 3, 5, 7). XPS spectra of BP and xBP-D (x = 3, 5, and 7): (c) Survey spectra and high resolution (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, and (f) O 1s spectra.

(a) HR-TEM images of 7BP-D sample. Insets are magnified images of the area marked by the yellow circle and the TEM image. (b, c) AFM images and (d, e) surface morphologies of the 3BP-D and 7BP-D samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra and (b) PL spectra of 3BP-D and 7BP-D samples excited at 320 nm. Inset of (a): Photographs of 3BP-D and 7BP-D samples and (b) irradiated with 365 nm light in a viewing cabinet.

(a) Selectivity for the detection of the metal cations Li+, Na+, K+, Sr2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Sn2+. The I/I
0 were values calculated from the PL emission intensity spectra at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. (b) Images of the 7BP-D solutions containing the various metal cations in a viewing cabinet under excitation at 365 nm or in daylight. The concentration of the metal cations in the 7BP-D aqueous solution was 100 ppm (10 mL).

S0 → S1 NTOs of C46, [C46–Li]+, [C46–Na]+, [C46–K]+, [C46–Ag]+, [C46–Sr]2+, [C46–Sn]2+, [C46–Fe]2+, [C46–Ni]2+, and [C46–Zn]2+calculated using the PBE38 functional with the 6–31g(d) basis set. Hole and ele represent the excited hole and electron, respectively.

Analysis of the S0 → S1 transitions of C46 and the C46-ion complexes. Ex, Δr, Λ, IFCT, and OS represent the excitation energy, excited electron-hole pair distance, excited electron-hole pair overlap, inter-fragment charge transfer, and oscillator strength. The last two columns are the percentage contribution of the ion to the excited hole and electron. Excited state calculations were carried out using the PBE38 functional with the 6–31g(d) basis set.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence quenching of thermally treated waste-derived carbon dots for selective metal ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Daan Dist., Taipei City, 106, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Tokyo University of Science, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Tokyo University of Science, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering & Innovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture & Research Center of Sustainable Energy and Nanotechnology, National Chung Hsing University, Kuo-Kuang Road, Taichung, 250, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Engineering & Innovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture & Research Center of Sustainable Energy and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering & Innovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture & Research Center of Sustainable Energy and Nanotechnology, National Chung Hsing University, Kuo-Kuang Road, Taichung, Taiwan 250

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Daan Dist., Taipei City, 106, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, carbon-dots (CDs) were derived from the thermal oxidation of an agricultural waste, bitter tea residue, to obtain different sp2/sp3 ratios and electronic structures for metal sensing. The CDs obtained from calcination at 700 °C exhibited the highest photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of 11.8% among all the samples treated at different temperatures. These CDs had a high degree of graphitization, which resulted in a strong π-π* electron transition, and hence in a high QY. The strong photoluminescence of the CDs could be used to sense the metal ions Ag+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Sn2+ by monitoring their PL intensity at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The metals inhibited the PL intensity in the order Ag+ > Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ > Sr2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, which demonstrated that the CDs exhibited high metal ion detection capability and selectivity. The detection of Fe3+ using CDs was performed in the range of 10–100 ppm with a LOD (limit of detection) value of 0.380 ppm. Theoretical calculations demonstrated that Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ induced charge transfer excitation and that Fe2+ and Ni2+ induced d-d transitions via complexation with the sp2 clusters. The charge transfer excitation and d-d transitions hindered the π-π* transition of the sp2 clusters, leading to a quenching effect. On the other hand, Li+, Na+, and K+ ions did not alter the π-π* transition of the sp2 clusters, resulting in a negligible quenching effect. In summary, the oxidation level and electronic structure of CDs derived from bitter tea residue could be tailored, and the CDs were shown to be a facile, sustainable, and eco-friendly material for metal sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: The fascinating electronic and optical properties of carbon-based dots have drawn considerable attention worldwide due to their potential in bio-labeling, sensing, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, catalysis, sensitizer, and electronics applications, among others (Adamo and Barone, 1999; Briscoe, 2015; Casida and Chong, 1995). Generally, carbon-based dots can be divided into two families: Graphene-based dots (GDs) and carbon dots (CDs) (Chen et al., 2017, 2018; Cheng et al., 2017). GDs derived from graphite have excellent electrical and thermal conductivity, mechanical strength, and optical properties, which has made them a popular research topic. GDs exhibit greater crystallinity than CDs because of the sp2-hybridized bonding within their aromatic units. GDs composed of a single graphite layer or a few graphite layers with dimensions smaller than a few nanometers have unique graphenic features (Das et al., 2018). GDs can be derived simply from graphite, graphene, graphene oxide, and other mixtures thereof. CDs with a hybridized carbon domain consisting of amorphous and crystalline parts can be produced from natural resources, plastics, and other derivatives of carbon-containing compounds (Dimiev and Tour, 2014). Interestingly, CDs and GDs can be functionalized with various chemical groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups, as a method of controlling their optical and electronic properties. Their photoluminescence (PL) and wavelength-dependent emission features are ascribed to the recombination of electron-hole pairs localized within the small sp2 carbon domains confined within an sp3 matrix (Eda et al., 2010; Ehrlich et al., 2011; Fan et al., 2014; Francl et al., 1982; Frisch et al., 2016). These results suggest that the determination of the sp2/sp3 configuration of CDs is extremely important to clarify the origin of the photoluminescence.

Carbon dots (CDs) are widely prepared from agricultural wastes and natural resources, which represents an alternative and value-added approach compatible with the concept of a circular economy (Guido et al., 2013; He et al., 2018; Hehre et al., 1972; Hu et al., 2018). The preparation of CDs derived from natural resources and agricultural wastes via bottom-up and top-down methods, including electrochemical, chemical, or physical techniques, has attracted worldwide attention (Hu et al., 2016). Bitter tea oil is a highly nutritious oil; the wastes resulting from its extraction contain mostly carbon, with small amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, phosphorus, and other metals. Thus, waste from bitter tea oil production could serve as a potential feedstock candidate for carbon dots. Wang et al. prepared activated carbon from bitter tea waste for the desalination of saltwater (Li et al., 2010).

Many attempts have been made to tailor the sp2/sp3 matrix and oxidation state of CDs to utilize the photoluminescence intensity of CDs for the efficient detection of heavy metals, which are harmful to ecosystems and living organisms. Generally, these heavy metals are non-biodegradable, toxic, and result in a high risk to the environment (Xia et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). For example, both excess iron accumulation and iron deficiency lead to serious health issues, including liver, kidney, and DNA damage. In this context, metal detection using CDs derived from natural resources has shown promise in bioimaging, labeling, and biochemical analyses, such as metabolism studies and anemia diagnosis (Li et al., 2012). Table S1 summarized the metal detection performance using CDs derived from different bio-related compounds. Metal ion detection can be achieved via the quenching effect of the metals on the CDs, which in turn is attributable to the chemical or physical bonding of the free metal ions to the CDs. The photoluminescence of CDs can be inhibited by photoinduced charge transfer and is easy to investigate (Li et al., 2015; Kang and Lee, 2019; Hu et al., 2019). As a consequence, the heavy metal detection performance of CDs is strongly associated with their structural, optical, and chemical properties, as well as their functionalization and the cluster size of the sp2 region (Lim et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013). However, to the best of our knowledge, no reports have been published regarding the preparation of CDs from bitter tea oil residues, the relationship between their crystallinity, optical properties, and photoluminescent heavy metal detection performance, or the correlation between the sp2 clusters of CDs and their quenching effects.

In this study, an agricultural waste, bitter tea oil residue, was utilized as a precursor to synthesize CDs via a thermal oxidation method at different temperatures. The correlation between the crystalline structure of the sp2/sp3 domains of the resulting CDs and their optical properties was investigated. CDs calcined at 700 °C exhibited a PL quantum yield of 11.8%, which was attributed to their strongly graphitic nature. Moreover, theoretical calculations based on density functional theory were performed and analyzed. Heavy metal detection experiments revealed that iron ions could be detected via their inhibition of the photoluminescence of the CDs. Our results suggest that CDs derived from bitter tea oil residue are easily prepared and can be considered one of the most effective tools for metal ion detection.

Bitter tea residue (BP) was obtained from a bitter tea oil factory in New Taipei City, Taiwan, and washed several times using ethanol solution before use. After washing, the bitter tea residue was ground thoroughly and then subjected to thermal oxidation in a tubular furnace at 300 °C, 500 °C, or 700 °C for 6 h (ramp rate was 5 °C min−1) under air atmosphere. The products were collected and are referred to as xBP, where x denotes the oxidation temperature (x = 3, 5, and 7, corresponding to 300 °C, 500 °C, and 700 °C, respectively). The products were then suspended in a deionized water solution and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min. The precipitate was removed from the solution to obtain a transparent suspension containing carbon dots (CDs). These suspensions are designated as xBP-D hereafter. An overview of the synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 a.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Tensor 27, Bruker, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; VG Scientific ESCALAB 250, Electron Spectroscopy for Chemical Analysis, UK) using Al Kα radiation were employed to examine the chemical functionalities and energy binding states of the samples. The X-ray photoelectron spectra were calibrated relative to the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. The degree of graphitization and crystalline structures of the samples were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) via a powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 ADVANCE, USA) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at a scan rate of 6° min−1. The surface morphologies of the samples were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Hitachi, H-7500, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM; Bruker, Dimension icon, USA). Raman spectra were recorded using a scanning monochromator (Jobin Yvon, with 1200 lines mm−1 holographic grating, blazed for 630 nm) to collect the signal obtained using a diode-pumped solid-state laser at an excitation line of 640 nm. Optical light absorption and photoluminescence (PL) properties were measured using a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV–Vis, Hitachi U-3900, Japan) and a Spex Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan) equipped with a 450 W Xe light source and double excitation monochromators. The luminescence color changes of the samples were observed using a viewing cabinet equipped with a UV lamp with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The quantum yields of the CDs were calculated using the equation below. Fluorescein and 0.1 M NaOH were employed as the standard and the solvent, respectively (Hu et al., 2018).
Qy (x) = (I x/I st)(η x/η st)(A x/A st)Qy (st)
Where I indicates the integrated luminescence intensity, η refers to the refractive index, A is the absorbance obtained from the UV–Vis spectra, and Qy corresponds to the quantum yield; the subscript x indicates the target compound, and st refers to the standard solution.

The optical properties of graphene oxides are governed by the size of their sp2 clusters (Lim et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013), and the PL quenching effect in metal sensing using graphene oxide may originate from changes in electronic structure when metal ions are coordinated to the graphene oxide surface. We used time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT)(Lu et al., 2017; Lu and Chen, 2012) to examine the influence of the size of the sp2 clusters on the optical properties of the carbon units. 7BP-D should have smaller sp2 clusters than 5BP-D and 3BP-D due to having undergone more oxidation. Therefore, we constructed C46, C60, and C114 clusters to represent the different sizes of the sp2 clusters in 7BP-D, 5BP-D, 3BP-D, respectively (see Fig. 1b). The PBE0-D3 functional (Mewada et al., 2013; Nair et al., 2017) with the 6–31g(d) (Ogren et al., 2009; Peach et al., 2008; Qu et al., 2012) basis set was implemented to obtain optimized ground state structures for C46, C60, C114, and the PBE38 functional was applied in further TDDFT calculations to examine the excited states. All theoretical calculations were carried out using Gaussian 16 Rev C.01 (Rassolov et al., 2001). Previous studies have indicated that the adsorption of metal ions on the surface of CDs results in a quenching effect in their PL spectra (Stratmann et al., 1998; Sun et al., 2013a, 2013b). Thus, we used TDDFT calculations to examine the low-lying excited states of C46 coordinated by a series of ions, namely, Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Sr2+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+, to explain the PL quenching of 7BP-D. The def2-svp (Sun et al., 2006) basis set was applied for complexes containing Ag+, Sr2+, or Sn2+ ions. Multiwfn was used to analyze the features of the low-lying excited states, such as their electron-hole pair distances, electron-hole pair overlaps, exciton binding energies, inter-fragment charge transfer (IFCT), and natural transition orbital (NTO) (Suo et al., 2017; Syshchyk et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2014). We selected first-row transition metal ions with an even number of electrons, such as Fe2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+, because TDDFT cannot handle open-shell systems properly (Tian et al., 2016). Moreover, we set the [C46–Fe]2+ and [C46–Ni]2+ complexes to have closed shells in the TDDFT calculations.

For fluorescence measurement, a fixed amount of an aqueous solution of 7BP-D was diluted to 1 mL using deionized water, and its fluorescence intensity at 420 nm upon excitation at a wavelength of 320 nm was measured as I
0 . PL spectra were recorded to determine the selectivity of metal ion detection by individually adding solutions of the metal ions Li+ (LiCl), Na+ (NaCl), K+ (KCl), Sr2+ (Sr(NO3)2), Ag+ (AgNO3), Fe2+ (FeSO4), Fe3+ (FeCl3), Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2), Cu2+ (CuCl2), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), and Sn2+ (SnI2). The detection of Fe3+ ions was performed using different concentrations (10, 25, 50, and 100 ppm) of Fe3+ solution by adding a fixed amount of 7BP-D. The PL intensity of the solution was measured at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

The structural features of the as-prepared BP and xBP were determined from their XRD patterns and FTIR spectra (Figure S1, Supporting Information). The XRD pattern of BP (Figure S1a) shows two peaks at 2θ values of approximately 15° and 22°. After thermal oxidation (xBP samples, x = 3, 5, 7), these peaks shift to lower and higher 2θ values, reaching approximately 11.5° and 28°, respectively. The shifts in these peaks suggested the presence of graphitic carbon (at ca. 28°), which originated in part from thermal oxidation. Figure S1b shows the FTIR spectra of the xBP samples, which confirm the presence of CC, C–C, and C–O (epoxy) groups (Walekar et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2012).

The xBP-D samples (the carbon dots obtained after centrifugation and filtration of the xBP suspensions) were then obtained. The surface functional groups of the BP and xBP-D samples were examined using FTIR (Fig. 2 a). The xBP-D samples exhibited intense peaks at 1638 and 2962 cm−1, which were ascribed to the CC bonds of the aromatic hydrocarbons (Walekar et al., 2018), and another peak corresponding to the C–O (epoxy) stretching vibration at 1260 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2012). The broad peaks in the spectra of the xBP-D samples at approximately 3000–3800 cm−1 originated from the –OH groups of water molecules adsorbed on the surface of the dots (Wang et al., 2014), as the samples were suspended in deionized water. In the spectra of BP, a C–O bond stretching band was observed at approximately 1000–1100 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2014), whereas the bands at approximately 1060 and 1748 cm−1 were attributed to the carboxylic groups (Walekar et al., 2018). The peaks at 2854 and 2926 cm−1 were assigned to the –C–H stretching modes of the methyl or methylene groups of aliphatic hydrocarbons (Wang et al., 2014). In the xBP-D spectra, these peaks vanished, while the intensity of the peaks corresponding to CC bonds (1638 and 2962 cm−1) increased, demonstrating that the aliphatic hydrocarbons were removed or interconnected to form aromatic units in the CDs (Walekar et al., 2018). All these results reveal that carbonization of the carboxylic groups occurred during the thermal oxidation of BP, forming stable CDs (xBP-D) with aromatic units containing epoxy groups on their basal plane.

The crystallinity and hybridization of BP and xBP-D were evaluated from their Raman spectra (Fig. 2b). Two strong peaks corresponding to the D- (sp3 hybridization) and G-band (sp2 hybridization) were observed at approximately 1349 and 1585 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2017). The BP sample exhibited the lowest sp2/sp3 hybridization, indicating that it had the most amorphous nature. The intensity ratios (ID/IG) of the 7BP-D, 5BP-D, and 3BP-D samples were estimated to be 0.95, 0.91, and 0.84, respectively, suggesting an increased sp3 carbon content and a greater level of graphitization with increasing thermal oxidation temperature.

Fig. 2c shows the survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples. The survey spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples are composed mainly of C 1s and O 1s, with minor N and S signals (Fig. 2c and Table S2). The nitrogen contents of these samples are lower than 2%, whereas the carbon and oxygen contents increased and decreased, respectively, with increasing thermal oxidation temperature (Table S2), indicating successful carbonization. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples can be fitted to three peaks at 284.7, 285.7, and 287.8 eV, which were assigned to C–C/CC bonds (sp2 and sp3 structure), C–O bonds, and OC–OH groups (carboxylate group) (Weigend and Ahlrichs, 2005), respectively (Fig. 2d). In agreement with the previous FTIR analysis, thermal oxidation was found to result in the formation of graphitic carbon domains to form CDs by interconnecting the carbonyl and carboxylate groups of BP. Among the xBP-D samples, 7BP-D exhibited the highest degree of graphitization (69.2% graphitic carbon and 30.8% oxygenated carbon), indicating its more organized sp2/sp3 connections and greater retention of carbon rings in the basal plane, which is advantageous for the π-π* electron transition and hence for achieving high conductivity (Yang et al., 2018; Yeh et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018). On the other hand, the small peak located at approximately 293 eV, which was assigned as a shake-up peak (characteristic of conjugated systems or aromatic groups) (Zhang et al., 2013a), increased with the thermal oxidation temperature. This result supports the above conclusion that the oxidation temperature can effectively improve the carbonization degree and hence stabilize the aromatic unit. Fig. 2e shows the high-resolution N 1s spectra, which reveals that only trace N was detected. Fig. 2f presents the high-resolution O 1s spectra of the BP and xBP-D samples. The fitted peaks at approximately 531.4 and 533.4 eV were attributed to CO and C–OH bonds, consistent with the above FTIR and high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Cheng et al., 2019). Based on the above analysis, the xBP-D obtained in this study exhibited a high degree of carbonization with many CC and C–C bonds, as well as a certain fraction of C–O (epoxy) groups, within its aromatic units. The functional groups attached to the surface of CDs and the degree of carbonization of the CDs were strongly correlated with their thermal oxidation temperature, which could thus be an important parameter to govern their PL emission and metal sensing properties.

The appearance and morphologies of the xBP-D samples were observed using TEM and AFM; selected images are presented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a shows that the particles 7BP-D had a size distribution of 10–20 nm and irregular shapes. The insets of Fig. 3a show high-resolution images, which reveal that 7BP-D has a distinct graphitic carbon nature. The TEM images of the 3BP-D and 5BP-D samples are shown in Figure S2. These samples aggregated to form large particles with sizes of approximately 30–50 nm. Many small and irregular particles are also observed near the 3BP-D aggregates, whereas such particles were not presented in the 5BP-D and 7BP-D samples. These small particles could be impurities originating from the bitter tea residue, and might be removed by thermal oxidation at a temperature higher than 500 °C. Additionally, the AFM images in Fig. 3b–e suggest that the 3BP-D particles were smaller and thinner than the 7BP-D particles. The height distributions of 3BP-D and 7BP-D were 1–4 and 3–10 nm, respectively, demonstrating that the xBP-D samples were few-layer graphitic carbon sheets.

Fig. 4 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the xBP-D samples. The absorption edges for 3BP-D and 7BP-D were located at 300 and 500 nm, respectively. A strong absorption could be observed for the 7BP-D sample at approximately 230 nm, which corresponds to a π electron transition from π → π* of CC bond in an aromatic domain (Zhang et al., 2013b); whereas an n-π* transition of a CO bond was found at around 280 nm for the 3BP-D sample. This result agrees with the above conclusion that the graphitization degree increased and the amount of oxygenated carbon decreased with increasing thermal oxidation temperature for the xBP-D samples. Theoretical calculations were further employed to investigate the optical properties of these samples. Table S3 shows the wavelengths corresponding to the strongest UV–Vis absorption as obtained from the theoretical calculations (345, 355, and 466 nm for C46 (7BP-D), C60 (5BP-D), and C114 (3BP-D), respectively). C46 and C60 had similar HOMO‒LUMO gaps (3.7 and 3.8 eV) and absorption wavelengths. C114 showed the longest absorption wavelength and lowest HOMO‒LUMO gap (3.12 eV) among the three molecular models because of its large sp2 cluster size. Additionally, the large sp2 cluster in C114 created significant transition dipole moments, leading to stronger absorption peaks than those of C46 and C60. The calculated absorption wavelengths and intensities for C46, C60, and C114 were consistent with the measured UV–Vis spectra of the 7BP-D and 3BP-D samples. The absorption peak in the UV–Vis spectrum of 3BP-D exhibited a redshift and greater intensity than that of 7BP-D (Fig. 4a). The exciton binding energies for the strongest UV–Vis absorptions of C46, C60, and C114 were 3.51, 3.30, and 2.53 eV, respectively. The low exciton binding energy of C114 indicates that the excited electron-hole pair in C114 is much easier to separate than those of C60 and C46. The large sp2 clusters in C114 facilitate electron separation upon photon excitations; thus, the excited electrons in C114 have less chance of returning to the ground state, resulting in the low PL intensity of 3BP-D (Lim et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013).

PL spectra were obtained to investigate the luminescence properties of the 3BP-D and 7BP-D samples (Figure S3). Both the 3BP-D and 7BP-D samples exhibited strong PL peaks at approximately 430–480 nm. Interestingly, excitation-dependent PL behavior was observed for these samples in the excitation wavelength range 280–380 nm. In the 3BP-D sample, as the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 380 nm, the PL peak increased and shifted toward a longer wavelength of approximately 480 nm, whereas the PL peak shifted to ~430 nm in the 3BP-D samples. Both samples exhibited significant and similar excitation dependence, in agreement with a previous report (Zheng et al., 2015). Excitation-dependent PL behavior can result from the synergy between quantum confinement (size effect) and chemical effects (i.e., trap states, surface functional groups, and edge states). In our study, the xBP-D particles were clearly larger than their quantum size, indicating that the chemical effects should play important roles in PL emission. Fig. 4b shows the PL emission spectra of the 3BP-D and 7BP-D samples excited at a wavelength of 320 nm. 7BP-D exhibited stronger PL emission than the 3BP-D sample. The inhibited PL emission of 3BP-D could be ascribed to non-radiative recombination and vibration relaxation due to its abundant surface functional groups, including C–OH and OC–OH, as observed in the FTIR and XPS analyses. Additionally, the high content of graphitic carbon in 7BP-D is advantageous to π-electron transitions within its aromatic units, leading to strong PL emission and UV–vis absorption at 230 nm (Fig. 4a). The inset of Fig. 4b shows photographs of aqueous solutions of 3BP-D and 7BP-D irradiated under 365 nm light in a viewing cabinet. Both samples exhibited blue emission; the emission of 7BP-D was brighter and stronger than that of 3BP-D. The PL quantum yields of 3BP-D and 7BP-D were calculated to be 2.01% and 11.8%, respectively. The blue emission of the xBP-D samples could be ascribed to the π-π* electron transition within the CC bonds in the sp2 domain of the aromatic units. The XPS, UV–Vis, FT-IR, Raman, and PL spectra all suggested that the luminescent properties of CDs are strongly governed by their C–C/CC bonds and hence by the sp2/sp3 carbon ratio in the aromatic units.

Theoretical calculations were also employed to elucidate the fluorescence of C46 (7BP-D), C60 (5BP-D), and C114 (3BP-D) based on Kasha’s rule, which states that photon emission occurs in appreciable yield from the lowest excited state of a given multiplicity. We carried out geometry optimizations of the lowest excited states of C46, C60, and C114. Table S3 shows the calculated fluorescence wavelengths and corresponding oscillator strengths. The oscillator strengths of the S1 → S0 transitions in C46 and C60 were greater than those of C114, indicating that the small sp2 clusters had stronger fluorescence intensity than the large sp2 clusters. The LUMO to HOMO transition dominated the fluorescence emission of C46 and C60, but the degeneracy of the frontier molecular orbitals of C114 contributed to LUMO to HOMO-1 and LUMO+1 to HOMO fluorescence emission. These two transitions of C114 canceled out the transition dipole moments, leading to weak fluorescence intensity. The calculation results are consistent with the experimental observation that 7BP-D, which had a small sp2 domain, exhibited stronger PL intensity than 3BP-D, which had a large sp2 cluster (Fig. 4b).

Iron ions are common and ubiquitous in our living system. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of the USA has developed limits for various contaminants, including iron cations, in drinking water in order to protect public health. Figure S4a shows the correlation between the luminescence intensity of 7BP-D and the concentration of Fe2+. The luminescence intensity decreased with increasing Fe2+ concentration, revealing that the Fe2+ ions adsorbed onto the surface of 7BP-D, where they acted as electron sinks to induce radiative recombination and inhibit the luminescence. The quenching effect was evaluated using the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation, I
0 /I = 1+ K
SV [M], where I
0 and I are the luminescence intensity before and after the addition of the metal ions, K
SV is the quenching effect constant, and [M] is the concentration of the metal ion (Li et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2012). The SV plot for the Fe3+ ion in Figure S4a shows a linear relationship, which implies that Fe3+ has a strong static/dynamic quenching effect on the luminescence (Zhang et al., 2013b). The binding constants and number of binding sites can be obtained using the equation log [(I
0 –I)/I] = logK + nlog [M], where K and n represent the binding constant and the number of the binding sites; the values of these parameters were calculated to be 507 and 0.5662, respectively, indicating strong binding and abundant binding sites for Fe3+ ions on 7BP-D (inset of Figure S4b).. (Zhang et al., 2013b)

To examine the selectivity of the detection of various metal cations by 7BP-D, the effect of the metal ions Li+, Na+, K+, Sr2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Sn2+ at a concentration of 100 ppm was evaluated (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b shows images of each of the solutions in a viewing cabinet under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm or in daylight. Remarkably, Ag+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ induced the greatest luminescence inhibition, decreasing the I/I
0 value to ~0.1, while Ni2+ and Cu2+ led to apparent luminescent quenching, and Sr2+, Co2+, and Sn2+ exhibited a slight quenching effect. However, the emission intensity was almost unchanged in the presence of Li+, Na+, K+, and Zn2+, implying that 7BP-D exhibits high selectivity toward these metal cations. Qu et al. suggested that such luminescence quenching could be attributed to nonradiative charge recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process (Zheng et al., 2015). The limit of detection (LOD) value is an indicator to determine the sample with a low level of analyte (Zhu et al., 2010), and is calculated to be approximately 0.380 ppm (Figure S5). Besides the metal cations, anion detection using CDs was rarely seen. Some carbon dot systems containing metal cation and anion, such as (Cu2+‒CN‒) (Dong et al., 2014), (Cu2+‒S2‒) (Hou et al., 2013), and (Cu2+‒P2O7 4−) (Wang et al., 2015), have been reported to exhibit an off-on phenomenon to probe different cation-anion pairs (Sun and Lei, 2017). It can be concluded that CDs-based materials would be desirable for the detection of different ionic compounds.

In other cases, such those of the Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ ions, the S0 → S1 transitions of the [C46–Ag]+, [C46–Sr]2+, and [C46–Sn]2+ complexes exhibited charge transfer. The NTOs indicated that the excited electron and hole were located on the metal ion and C46, respectively, in [C46–Ag]+, [C46–Sr]2+, and [C46–Sn]2+ (Fig. 6). As listed in Table 1, 0.914, 0.991, and 0.779e of charge were transferred from C46 to the Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ ions, respectively. Moreover, over 80% of the excited electron was located on the Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ ions. Compared to [C46–Li]+, [C46–Na]+, and [46-K]+, [C46–Ag]+, [C46–Sr]2+, and [C46–Sn]2+ exhibited larger excited electron-hole pair distances (2–3 Å) and lower excited electron-hole pair overlaps (0.25–0.35). When Sr2+, Sn2+, and Ag+ were coordinated to the C46 surface, the local π-π* excitation of C46 disappeared, and the S0 → S1 transition occurred via charge transfer excitation from C46 to the metal ions. The charge transfer excitation blocked further fluorescence emission from the C46 cluster, causing the PL quenching effect. The calculation results were consistent with the experimentally observed PL quenching effect of 7BP-D in the presence of Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ ions (Fig. 6).

The [C46–Fe]2+, [C46–Ni]2+, and [C46–Zn]2+ complexes exhibited low excitation energy in their S0 → S1 transitions. The amounts of charge transfer between C46 and the Fe2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ ions were smaller than those of [C46–Sr]2+, [C46–Sn]2+, and [C46–Ag]+. Based on the orbital analysis and NTOs of the S0 → S1 transitions, the electron-hole pair was partially located on the Fe2+ and Ni2+ ions in the [C46–Fe]2+ and [C46–Ni]2+ complexes (Fig. 6). The d-d transitions of the metal ion became dominant in the [C46–Fe]2+ and [C46–Ni]2+ complexes rather than the π-π* transition of the C46 cluster. As a result, the fluorescence emission of the C46 cluster was inhibited by the coordination of Fe2+ and Ni2+. These calculation results are consistent with the PL quenching effect observed for 7BP-D in the presence of Fe2+ and Ni2+ ions (Fig. 5). We believe that the same mechanism explains the PL quenching effect of 7BP-D in the detection of Fe3+, Co2+, and Cu2+ ions. The S0 → S1 transition of the [C46–Zn]2+ complex was found to exhibit charge‒transfer character based on wavefunction analysis. The C46 cluster transferred 0.294 e to Zn2+ during the S0 → S1 transition. The calculation results suggested a quenching effect should be observed for 7BP-D in the presence of Zn2+, but no such effect was observed in the experiment. This discrepancy may have occurred because the 7BP-D sample in the test was not able to bind the Zn2+ ions effectively.

In this study, carbon dots derived from bitter tea residue were successfully prepared and enabled the selective detection of metal cations. The 7BP-D sample prepared at 700 °C had the highest quantum yield (11.8%) and a LOD value to detect Fe3+ of 0.380 ppm among the samples in our study, which was attributed to its high degree of graphitization, retention of aromatic rings in the basal plane, and strong π-π* electron transition. In addition, the 7BP-D sample exhibited high metal ion detection capability via the selective capture of the metal ions Ag+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Sn2+ species. Theoretical calculations demonstrated that the Li+, Na+, and K+ ions did not alter the π-π* transition of the sp2 clusters, and thus did not inhibit their fluorescence emission. On the contrary, Ag+, Sr2+, and Sn2+ ions induced transfer excitation, and Fe2+ and Ni2+ ions showed d-d transition when coordinated to sp2 clusters. The charge transfer excitation and d-d transition inhibited the fluorescence emission of the cluster, resulting in a PL quenching effect. It is expected that carbon dots prepared from bitter tea residue, with their strong luminescence, highly selective metal ion detection, and easy fabrication will have the wide applications in bio-imaging, bioscience, and bio-labeling.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
